questions.q: What are shadow properties in EF Core and when would you use them?

answer.oneLiner: Shadow properties are columns that exist in the EF model and the database but have no matching C# property on the class. You read and write them through the change tracker API.
answer.detail: When you call modelBuilder.Entity<Order>().Property<DateTime>("LastModified"), EF creates a column in the Orders table but the Order class has no LastModified property. The value lives in the EntityEntry inside the change tracker. This is useful when you want audit columns like CreatedAt or TenantId on the database without polluting the domain class with infrastructure concerns. EF also creates shadow properties automatically for conventional foreign keys when you define a navigation property but skip the FK property. To reference a shadow property inside a LINQ query you use EF.Property<T>(entity, "PropertyName"), which EF translates to the real column in SQL.
keyPoints: Defined in OnModelCreating with Property<T>("Name") and have no C# class member.
Great for audit columns like CreatedAt or LastModified you do not want on the domain model.
Access and set values via ctx.Entry(e).Property("Name").CurrentValue.
Reference in LINQ queries with EF.Property<T>(entity, "Name") so EF translates to SQL.
EF also auto-creates shadow FK properties when you add navigations without FK properties.

code.note: This demonstrates a clean domain class using shadow properties for audit columns, and shows how to set and query those columns through the EF API.

answer.analogy: A shadow property is a back-office column the customer never sees but the system always tracks.
redFlags: Saying shadow properties live on the C# class - they exist only in the EF model and change tracker.
Not knowing you access them via ctx.Entry(e).Property("Name").CurrentValue.
Confusing shadow properties with private C# properties - they are completely different things.
Not knowing you use EF.Property<T>() to reference them inside LINQ queries.

followUpsQA.q: How does EF use shadow properties for foreign keys automatically?
followUpsQA.a: If you define a navigation property without a matching FK property, EF creates a shadow property for the FK column so the relationship still works.

followUpsQA.q: How do you set a shadow property value in a SaveChanges interceptor?
followUpsQA.a: Use ctx.Entry(entity).Property("LastModified").CurrentValue = value inside an override of SaveChanges or a SaveChangesInterceptor.

followUpsQA.q: Can you query on a shadow property in a LINQ Where clause?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, use EF.Property<T>(entity, "PropertyName") inside the lambda and EF translates it to the correct column in SQL.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between a shadow property and a private C# backing field?
followUpsQA.a: A private backing field still belongs to the C# class; a shadow property has no C# member at all and exists only in the EF metadata and change tracker.

dialogue.text: What are shadow properties in EF Core, and when would you reach for them?

They are EF model properties that map to a real database column but have no matching C# member on the class. You configure them in OnModelCreating with Property<DateTime>("LastModified") and read or write them through the change tracker via Entry(e).Property("LastModified").CurrentValue.
What is a good real-world reason to use one instead of just adding the property to the class?

Audit columns like CreatedAt, LastModified, or TenantId. I want that data in the database for compliance, but the domain Invoice or Order class has no business knowing about infrastructure concerns. Shadow properties keep the domain clean without losing the column.
How do you actually filter a query on a shadow property? You cannot reference it from the C# class.
I use EF.Property<DateTime>(i, "LastModified") inside the LINQ lambda - that is the API EF provides for exactly this case, and it translates to the real column name in the generated SQL.
You mentioned EF creates shadow properties automatically. When does that happen?

When you define a navigation property but do not add a matching FK property to the class - for example, an Order with a Customer navigation but no CustomerId property. EF creates a shadow CustomerId column so the relationship still works in the database.
Can shadow properties be used inside a SaveChanges interceptor to auto-stamp all entities with an audit date?

Exactly - I override SaveChanges or implement ISaveChangesInterceptor, loop over ChangeTracker.Entries() filtered by state, and set Entry(e).Property("LastModified").CurrentValue = DateTime.UtcNow for each. One central place, no pollution on any domain class.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you implement soft deletes in EF Core using global query filters?

answer.oneLiner: Add an IsDeleted flag to the entity and register a global query filter with HasQueryFilter so EF automatically appends WHERE IsDeleted = 0 to every query for that entity type.
answer.detail: You configure HasQueryFilter(p => !p.IsDeleted) in OnModelCreating or in an IEntityTypeConfiguration class. From that point, every query against Products - whether a direct DbSet query, an Include, or a raw LINQ - will have the filter injected into the SQL automatically. To actually perform soft deletes you override SaveChanges (or use a SaveChangesInterceptor) and scan the ChangeTracker for entries in Deleted state. For each one you flip the state to Modified and set IsDeleted = true, so EF emits an UPDATE instead of a DELETE. When an admin needs to see deleted rows you call IgnoreQueryFilters() on that specific query, which bypasses all global filters for that query. Filters also compose - if you have a multi-tenancy filter and a soft-delete filter on the same entity, EF ANDs them together automatically.
keyPoints: HasQueryFilter(e => !e.IsDeleted) auto-injects WHERE IsDeleted = 0 into every query for the entity.
Override SaveChanges to intercept Deleted state entries and flip them to Modified with IsDeleted = true.
Use IgnoreQueryFilters() on a query to bypass the filter, for example in an admin restore endpoint.
Multiple filters on the same entity combine with AND - soft-delete and multi-tenancy stack cleanly.
Filters also apply to navigations included via Include(), which can hide related rows unexpectedly.

code.note: This shows the full pattern: one-time filter registration, automatic interception of deletes in SaveChanges, and how to bypass for admin scenarios.

answer.analogy: A global query filter is a bouncer at the door - deleted rows never get into any party unless you have the admin override pass.
redFlags: Hard-deleting rows when the business requirement is soft delete and data must be recoverable.
Not knowing IgnoreQueryFilters() exists and instead building a separate repository method.
Confusing global query filters with Where() clauses added manually on every query.
Not realising filters apply to navigations included via Include() and can cause unexpected missing rows.

followUpsQA.q: How do filters interact with Include and required navigations?
followUpsQA.a: The filter applies to the included entity type, so a required navigation pointing to a soft-deleted row can silently drop the parent from results - be careful with required relationships.

followUpsQA.q: How do you implement the soft-delete intercept without overriding SaveChanges on every DbContext?
followUpsQA.a: Use a SaveChangesInterceptor implementing ISaveChangesInterceptor and register it via options.AddInterceptors - it applies globally without touching the DbContext class.

followUpsQA.q: Can you scope a global query filter to a runtime value like a tenantId?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - inject a service or use a field on the DbContext in the lambda, for example HasQueryFilter(p => p.TenantId == _currentTenantId), where _currentTenantId is set from DI.

followUpsQA.q: Do global query filters apply when you use FromSqlRaw?
followUpsQA.a: No - raw SQL queries bypass global filters, so you must add the WHERE condition manually in the SQL string.

dialogue.text: Walk me through how you would implement soft deletes in EF Core.

I add an IsDeleted bool to the entity, then register a global query filter with HasQueryFilter(p => !p.IsDeleted) in the entity configuration. From that point EF automatically injects WHERE IsDeleted = 0 into every query for that type - no manual filtering in every repository method.
How do you make sure calling DbSet.Remove() does a soft delete and not a real DELETE?

I override SaveChanges in the DbContext and scan ChangeTracker.Entries for any entry in Deleted state that implements my ISoftDelete interface. I flip the state to Modified and set IsDeleted = true, so EF emits an UPDATE instead of a DELETE.
An admin screen needs to show deleted products and restore them. How do you handle the filter?

I call IgnoreQueryFilters() on that specific query - it bypasses all global filters for that query only. Then to restore I fetch the deleted product via that query, set IsDeleted = false, and call SaveChanges.
You have a multi-tenancy filter and a soft-delete filter on Product. Do they conflict?

No - EF combines multiple filters with AND. But you can only call HasQueryFilter once per entity type, so I combine them into a single lambda: HasQueryFilter(p => !p.IsDeleted && p.TenantId == _tenantId).
Any risk with filters and Include?

Yes - the filter applies to the included entity type too. If an Order includes a required Product that is soft-deleted, EF drops the whole Order from results. That surprises developers, so I always document it and test it explicitly.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you optimize bulk insert, update, and delete operations in EF Core?

answer.oneLiner: Avoid loading and tracking thousands of entities. Use ExecuteUpdate and ExecuteDelete from EF Core 7 for set-based SQL changes, and EFCore.BulkExtensions or SqlBulkCopy for high-volume inserts.
answer.detail: The default EF pattern - load entities, modify in memory, call SaveChanges - is fine for small numbers of rows. At scale it breaks down: every loaded entity takes an EntityEntry and a snapshot, DetectChanges is O(n), and SaveChanges in a loop means one round-trip per row. EF Core 7 added ExecuteUpdateAsync and ExecuteDeleteAsync, which run a single parameterized UPDATE or DELETE SQL statement directly without loading anything or touching the ChangeTracker. For bulk inserts the same problem exists: AddRange plus SaveChanges is slow at scale because EF batches but still does row-by-row parameter binding. EFCore.BulkExtensions provides BulkInsertAsync which uses SqlBulkCopy under the hood for maximum throughput. The key caveat with ExecuteUpdate and ExecuteDelete is that they bypass the ChangeTracker, so any entities already loaded in the same context will be stale in memory after the SQL runs.
keyPoints: EF Core 7+ ExecuteUpdateAsync and ExecuteDeleteAsync run a single SQL statement with no tracking and no SaveChanges call.
For large inserts, use EFCore.BulkExtensions BulkInsertAsync or SqlBulkCopy directly - AddRange plus SaveChanges does not scale.
SaveChanges in a loop is one round-trip per row even with batching and must be avoided at scale.
ExecuteUpdate and ExecuteDelete bypass the ChangeTracker, so tracked entities become stale in memory after the call.
Always run ExecuteUpdate or ExecuteDelete before loading related entities in the same request, or reload after.

code.note: ExecuteUpdate and ExecuteDelete do not go through SaveChanges and do not update in-memory tracked entities - call them before loading data or reload the context afterward.

answer.analogy: Bulk operations are a forklift - one trip for a thousand boxes, not a thousand trips for one box each.
redFlags: Looping SaveChanges per row for tens of thousands of rows and calling it acceptable.
Not knowing EF Core 7 added ExecuteUpdate and ExecuteDelete for set-based changes.
Not warning that ExecuteUpdate leaves tracked in-memory entities stale after it runs.
Reaching for AddRange and SaveChanges for a 100,000 row import without mentioning SqlBulkCopy.

followUpsQA.q: Why do loaded entities go stale after you call ExecuteUpdate?
followUpsQA.a: ExecuteUpdate runs SQL directly on the database without touching the in-memory ChangeTracker, so tracked entities still hold the old values until you reload them.

followUpsQA.q: When would you use raw SqlBulkCopy instead of EFCore.BulkExtensions?
followUpsQA.a: When you need maximum raw insert throughput with full control over batch size and column mappings, and you want zero third-party dependency in the data layer.

followUpsQA.q: Does ExecuteDelete fire soft-delete logic in SaveChanges?
followUpsQA.a: No - it bypasses SaveChanges entirely and sends a raw DELETE statement, so any soft-delete logic in your SaveChanges override or interceptor is skipped.

followUpsQA.q: How does EF Core batch SaveChanges calls, and where does it still fall short?
followUpsQA.a: EF batches multiple INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE statements into fewer round-trips, but each row still requires individual parameter binding - it does not use bulk copy, so throughput is limited at very high volumes.

dialogue.text: We need to reprice 80,000 products in a nightly job. How would you do that in EF Core?

I would not load 80,000 entities. In EF Core 7 I would use ExecuteUpdateAsync with a SetProperty call to multiply the Price column - that emits one parameterized UPDATE statement, no tracking, no SaveChanges, done in milliseconds.
What about a nightly import that inserts 50,000 new orders from a flat file?

AddRange plus SaveChanges will not scale here - it still does row-by-row parameter binding. I would use EFCore.BulkExtensions BulkInsertAsync, which uses SqlBulkCopy under the hood and can insert 50,000 rows in a few seconds.
After you run ExecuteUpdate, another part of the same request loads products and sees old prices. Why?

ExecuteUpdate bypasses the ChangeTracker entirely - it runs raw SQL on the database but does not update any in-memory entities. Any tracked Product loaded before the call will still show the old price. I always run ExecuteUpdate before loading data in the same request, or clear the tracker and reload.
The nightly job also needs to hard-delete audit logs older than 90 days. Same approach?

Exactly - ctx.AuditLogs.Where(l => l.CreatedAt < cutoff).ExecuteDeleteAsync() sends one DELETE statement. One important caveat: this bypasses SaveChanges, so any soft-delete interceptor logic is skipped. For hard deletes that is fine, but worth being aware of.
If EF Core 6 is the constraint and ExecuteUpdate is not available, what is your plan?

I fall back to ExecuteSqlInterpolatedAsync with a parameterized UPDATE statement directly. It is less fluent but achieves the same set-based result without loading entities. Alternatively, a stored procedure called via ExecuteSqlRaw gives the same throughput.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is the difference between Code First, Database First, and Migrations in EF Core?

answer.oneLiner: Code First means you define the schema in C# and EF generates the database. Database First scaffolds C# entities from an existing database. Migrations is not a separate approach - it is the tool that evolves the schema incrementally in a Code First workflow.
answer.detail: In Code First you write C# entity classes and configure them with Fluent API or Data Annotations. EF computes the schema from the model. Migrations then version those schema changes as C# migration files with Up() and Down() methods, tracked in __EFMigrationsHistory. In Database First you start with an existing SQL Server or Postgres schema and run dotnet ef dbcontext scaffold to generate entity classes and a DbContext. You are not the source of truth - the database is. The common confusion is treating Migrations as a third approach equal to Code First and Database First. It is not - Migrations is the change management mechanism inside Code First. EF Core removed the EDMX designer from EF6, so Code First plus Migrations is now the mainstream path for greenfield projects.
keyPoints: Code First: C# model is the source of truth, schema is generated from it via Migrations.
Database First: existing database is the source of truth, scaffold with dotnet ef dbcontext scaffold.
Migrations is not a third approach - it is how Code First versions and applies schema changes.
EF Core has no EDMX designer - Code First plus Migrations is the standard greenfield path.
You can scaffold Database First once and then switch to Code First Migrations going forward.

code.note: EnsureCreated is fine for tests and quick demos but cannot be used with Migrations - once Migrations exist, use MigrateAsync or the CLI instead.

answer.analogy: Code First is writing a script then performing a play; Database First is watching a play then writing down the script.
redFlags: Treating Migrations as a third approach separate from Code First and Database First.
Not knowing EF Core has no EDMX designer - EF6 experience can cause this confusion.
Saying Code First cannot work against an existing database - it can, with a baseline migration.
Using EnsureCreated in a project that also uses Migrations - they conflict.

followUpsQA.q: Can you use Migrations after starting Database First?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - after scaffolding, create a baseline migration that matches the existing schema, then continue evolving with Code First Migrations from that point.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between EnsureCreated and MigrateAsync?
followUpsQA.a: EnsureCreated creates the schema from the current model snapshot but cannot evolve it - it conflicts with Migrations. MigrateAsync applies pending migrations and is the production-safe choice.

followUpsQA.q: Why did EF Core remove the EDMX designer from EF6?
followUpsQA.a: EF Core was redesigned from scratch around code-based modeling and CLI tooling rather than XML designers and Visual Studio IDE integration.

followUpsQA.q: How do you stop re-scaffolding from overwriting custom logic in your entities?
followUpsQA.a: Use partial classes to put custom logic in a separate file that the scaffold command does not touch, or keep customizations in separate hand-written files.

dialogue.text: Explain the difference between Code First, Database First, and Migrations in EF Core.

Code First means C# is the source of truth - I define entities and config, and EF generates the database schema. Database First means an existing database is the source of truth - I scaffold C# entities from it using dotnet ef dbcontext scaffold. Migrations is not a third approach - it is the change-management tool inside Code First.
So where does Migrations fit exactly?

Every time I change the C# model I run dotnet ef migrations add to generate an Up/Down file that captures the diff. Then dotnet ef database update applies it. EF tracks applied migrations in __EFMigrationsHistory. That is Code First - Migrations just versions the schema changes.
A new developer coming from EF6 asks where the EDMX designer is. What do you tell them?

It does not exist in EF Core - the team removed it entirely. EF Core is code-first by design with no XML designer. The equivalent for starting from an existing database is dotnet ef dbcontext scaffold, which generates the C# code directly.
The team scaffolded an existing logistics database six months ago. Now they want to add new tables in C#. Can they switch to Migrations?

Yes - I would create a baseline migration that tells EF the current schema already exists. I create a migration with an empty Up() body and mark it as applied in __EFMigrationsHistory. From that point new changes generate new migrations as normal Code First.
What is wrong with calling EnsureCreated in a project that already uses Migrations?

They conflict - EnsureCreated creates the schema directly from the model without writing to __EFMigrationsHistory, so Migrations thinks no migrations have run and will try to apply them again. You use one or the other, never both.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: Owned entities vs value objects vs shadow properties — how do these concepts differ in EF Core?

answer.oneLiner: A value object is a DDD domain concept with no identity. An owned entity is EF Core's mechanism to map a multi-field value object to columns. A shadow property is a single mapped column with no C# member at all.
answer.detail: These three concepts are often confused because they all relate to things that are not standard top-level entities with their own DbSet. A value object in domain-driven design has no independent identity - two addresses with the same street and city are equal regardless of any ID. EF maps multi-field value objects using OwnsOne or OwnsMany. The owned type has its columns stored in the owner's table by default, has no primary key of its own, and cannot be queried through a DbSet. A shadow property is more specific - it is a single column in the database that has no corresponding property on any C# class. It exists only in the EF model metadata and the ChangeTracker. You use it for audit fields or tenant IDs you want in the database but not on the domain model. The key distinction: owned entities map multi-field objects, shadow properties map single hidden columns. A value object with three fields like Address (Street, City, PostCode) needs an owned entity. A single audit date column needs a shadow property.
keyPoints: Value object is a DDD concept - no identity, equality by value, not an EF concept itself.
Owned entity (OwnsOne/OwnsMany) is EF's mechanism to map a multi-field value object to columns in the owner's table.
Shadow property is a single column mapped by EF with no corresponding C# property on the class.
Use owned types for multi-field value objects like Address; use shadow properties for single hidden columns like audit dates.
Owned types have no DbSet and cannot be queried independently; shadow properties are accessed via the ChangeTracker.

code.note: Owned entities map complex multi-field types to owner-table columns; shadow properties map single hidden columns not exposed on the class.

answer.analogy: Owned entity is a wallet built into the jacket; shadow property is a hidden pocket only the tailor knows about.
redFlags: Equating value object and owned entity as if they are the same thing - one is a design concept, the other is an EF mapping.
Thinking a shadow property can hold a multi-field type like Address - shadow properties are single columns only.
Trying to query owned entities through a DbSet - they have none.
Confusing OwnsOne with a regular one-to-one relationship that has a foreign key.

followUpsQA.q: How do you map a value object as a single column instead of multiple columns?
followUpsQA.a: Use a value converter to serialize the value object to a JSON string or a formatted string in one column, instead of using OwnsOne.

followUpsQA.q: Can an owned entity contain shadow properties?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - inside the OwnsOne builder lambda you can call Property<DateTime>("CreatedAt") to add a shadow property to the owned type's columns.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between a regular one-to-one navigation and an owned entity?
followUpsQA.a: A regular one-to-one has its own table, primary key, and DbSet. An owned entity has no primary key, no DbSet, and lives in the owner's table by default - it has no independent identity.

followUpsQA.q: How do you handle a null owned entity in EF Core?
followUpsQA.a: If all columns are null EF materializes the owned reference as null; for required owned types configure the properties as required so null is prevented at the database level.

dialogue.text: Can you explain the difference between owned entities, value objects, and shadow properties in EF Core?

Value object is a DDD domain concept - no identity, equality by value. EF's owned entity is the mapping mechanism for value objects: OwnsOne or OwnsMany maps the fields into the owner's table with no primary key and no DbSet. A shadow property is separate - it is a single database column with no C# member on the class, accessed only through the ChangeTracker.
So when do you choose owned entity versus shadow property?

Owned entity when the value object has multiple fields - like an Address with Street, City, and PostCode. Shadow property when I need a single hidden column, like a LastModified date or TenantId, that I do not want polluting the domain class.
A colleague wants to use a shadow property to map the whole Address type as one column. Is that possible?

No - a shadow property is a single column only. To map Address as one column I would use a value converter to serialize it to JSON and store it in one string column. For proper relational mapping with separate columns, OwnsOne is the right tool.
Can you query owned entities directly through a DbSet?

No - owned types have no DbSet. They load with the owner entity. That is the key difference from a regular related entity: you cannot do ctx.Addresses.Where(...) because Address has no independent identity and no registration in the model as a root entity.
How would you map an Address value object as a single column instead of three separate columns?

I would configure a value converter on a string property using HasConversion, serializing to and from JSON. One column in the database, full Address object in memory. No OwnsOne needed, but you lose the ability to query on individual address fields in SQL.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you handle a transaction that spans multiple SaveChanges calls or multiple operations in EF Core?

answer.oneLiner: A single SaveChanges call is already wrapped in a transaction automatically. To span multiple SaveChanges calls or operations, use Database.BeginTransactionAsync() and commit explicitly, rolling back on any exception.
answer.detail: By default EF Core wraps every SaveChanges call in its own transaction automatically - you do not need to do anything for a single atomic save. The issue arises when you need two or more SaveChanges calls to be atomic together, for example creating an Order and then a Payment in separate steps. You call ctx.Database.BeginTransactionAsync() to get an IDbContextTransaction, run your SaveChanges calls inside it, and call CommitAsync when all succeed. If any step throws you call RollbackAsync (or just let the using block dispose the uncommitted transaction). For ambient transactions that span multiple DbContext instances or cross-resource operations, you use TransactionScope. The important edge case is with resilience policies: if you have EnableRetryOnFailure configured, the retrying strategy needs to replay the entire transaction on a transient failure. Wrapping manual transactions inside ctx.Database.CreateExecutionStrategy().ExecuteAsync(...) ensures the strategy replays the whole block, not just the failing command.
keyPoints: A single SaveChanges is already transactional - EF wraps it automatically.
Use ctx.Database.BeginTransactionAsync() then CommitAsync to span multiple SaveChanges calls.
Always commit in the happy path; the using block rolls back if CommitAsync is never called.
Use TransactionScope for ambient transactions across multiple DbContext instances or resources.
With EnableRetryOnFailure, wrap the whole manual transaction in Database.CreateExecutionStrategy().ExecuteAsync(...).

code.note: Without execution strategy wrapping, EnableRetryOnFailure can retry only the failing command inside the transaction, not the whole transaction from the start.

answer.analogy: A transaction is a contract - sign everything or shred everything; there is no partial signing.
redFlags: Forgetting to call CommitAsync so the transaction silently rolls back at the end of the using block.
Using a manual transaction with EnableRetryOnFailure without wrapping it in CreateExecutionStrategy().ExecuteAsync.
Confusing TransactionScope ambient transactions with DbContext.Database.BeginTransaction.
Calling SaveChanges outside the transaction scope and thinking it is still atomic.

followUpsQA.q: What happens if you forget to call CommitAsync inside a using block?
followUpsQA.a: When the using block exits, Dispose is called on the IDbContextTransaction, which rolls back the uncommitted transaction - all changes are lost silently.

followUpsQA.q: Why does EnableRetryOnFailure conflict with manual transactions?
followUpsQA.a: The retry strategy replays the failing command, not the whole transaction. If the transaction is partially complete, replaying one command leaves it in an inconsistent state. You must wrap the full transaction block in ExecutionStrategy.ExecuteAsync so a retry restarts everything from the beginning.

followUpsQA.q: When would you choose TransactionScope over BeginTransaction?
followUpsQA.a: When the transaction must span two different DbContext instances, or cross a resource boundary like a message queue and a database, TransactionScope provides an ambient transaction that both can enlist in.

followUpsQA.q: Is it safe to call BeginTransaction and then read data before writing?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - reads inside a transaction use the same isolation level as the transaction, so you get a consistent snapshot. Just be aware of locking implications at higher isolation levels like Serializable.

dialogue.text: In our e-commerce app, creating an order and charging the customer are two separate operations. How do you make them atomic in EF Core?

I open an explicit transaction with ctx.Database.BeginTransactionAsync(), run SaveChanges for the order, run SaveChanges for the payment record, then call CommitAsync. If anything throws, the using block disposes the transaction and both changes roll back automatically.
A developer forgot to call CommitAsync but the code compiled and ran without errors. What happened to the data?

Nothing was saved. When the using block exited, Dispose was called on the IDbContextTransaction, which rolled back the uncommitted transaction. The order and payment records are gone. This is why I always make CommitAsync the last explicit line before the closing brace.
The team has EnableRetryOnFailure configured for transient Azure SQL errors. Does your manual transaction still work?

Not safely as-is. The retrying strategy replays the failing command, not the whole transaction block. I need to wrap everything inside ctx.Database.CreateExecutionStrategy().ExecuteAsync(...) so a transient failure triggers a complete restart of the transaction from the beginning.
What if the order and payment live in two completely separate DbContext instances?

BeginTransaction only covers one context. For two contexts I would use TransactionScope, which provides an ambient transaction both can enlist in. On Azure SQL it may escalate to a distributed transaction, so I would evaluate whether the performance cost is acceptable or redesign to avoid the cross-context write.
Is there a way to avoid the two-SaveChanges pattern entirely for this scenario?

Yes - if I add the Payment as a navigation property on Order and add both to the context before a single SaveChanges, EF inserts them together in the same automatic transaction. I only need an explicit transaction when I genuinely need multiple separate commits to be atomic.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: When should you use AsSplitQuery versus a single query for multiple Includes?

answer.oneLiner: A single query JOINs everything in one round-trip but multiplies rows when you include multiple collections. AsSplitQuery issues one SELECT per collection, avoiding the row explosion at the cost of extra round-trips and a potential consistency gap.
answer.detail: When you use Include on a collection navigation, EF generates a LEFT JOIN. With one collection this is fine. With two or more collections EF adds two JOINs, and the result set becomes a Cartesian product - if an Order has 10 Items and 10 StatusHistory records, the join returns 100 rows for what is really 10 + 10 = 20 entities. EF has to deduplicate in memory. AsSplitQuery tells EF to issue a separate SELECT per collection and stitch the results together in memory. This reduces rows dramatically but adds round-trips. The critical caveat is consistency: because the separate SELECTs are independent statements (not inside one transaction by default), rows can be inserted, updated, or deleted between them. For financial or audit data where a consistent snapshot matters, use a single query or wrap the split query in an explicit transaction. You can set the splitting behavior globally via UseQuerySplittingBehavior in DbContext options, and override per query with AsSplitQuery() or AsSingleQuery().
keyPoints: One collection Include is fine as a single query - no Cartesian product.
Two or more collection Includes cause Cartesian explosion - 10 x 10 = 100 rows for 20 entities.
AsSplitQuery issues one SELECT per collection and stitches in memory - fewer rows but more round-trips.
Split queries have a consistency risk - data can change between the separate SELECTs.
For financial or audit reads use a single query or wrap the split in an explicit transaction.

code.note: AsSplitQuery reduces rows and memory significantly for multiple collection Includes, but requires a transaction if you need a consistent snapshot across all the separate SELECTs.

answer.analogy: Single query is one courier delivering all packages at once; split query is separate couriers per package type - faster per trip but they leave at different times.
redFlags: Always using a single query with multiple collection Includes and not noticing the 100x row duplication.
Recommending AsSplitQuery without mentioning the consistency gap between separate SELECTs.
Confusing split queries with database partitioning or sharding.
Not knowing you can set the splitting behavior globally and override per query.

followUpsQA.q: How do you handle the consistency risk in split queries for financial data?
followUpsQA.a: Wrap the split query in an explicit transaction with BeginTransactionAsync so all the separate SELECTs execute within the same snapshot isolation level.

followUpsQA.q: How do you set split query as the global default?
followUpsQA.a: Call UseQuerySplittingBehavior(QuerySplittingBehavior.SplitQuery) inside the DbContext options configuration in your DI setup.

followUpsQA.q: How do you override the global split setting for a specific query that needs a single JOIN?
followUpsQA.a: Call AsSingleQuery() on that specific query - it overrides the global default for that one query only.

followUpsQA.q: Does AsSplitQuery work with pagination via Skip and Take?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, but you must have a stable OrderBy clause so the separate SELECTs for the collections use the same ordered set of parent rows - without it the pages may not align.

dialogue.text: You are loading a list of shipments with their Items and StatusHistory collections included. The query is returning way more rows than expected. What is happening?

That is Cartesian explosion. EF generates a LEFT JOIN for each collection Include. If a Shipment has 20 Items and 15 StatusHistory records, the JOIN produces 20 times 15 = 300 rows for what is really 35 entities. EF deduplicates in memory, but you are transferring 300 rows over the wire.
How do you fix it?

I add AsSplitQuery() to the query. EF then issues three separate SELECT statements - one for Shipments, one for Items, one for StatusHistory - and stitches the results together in memory. Total rows transferred drops from 300 down to 35.
Are there any downsides to split queries you would want me to know about?

Two main ones. First, extra round-trips - three SELECTs instead of one, which matters on high-latency connections. Second, consistency: the three SELECTs are independent statements, so a row can be inserted or deleted between them. For a shipping dashboard that is usually fine; for a financial settlement report I would wrap them in a transaction.
The team wants to make split query the default for the whole application. How?

In the DbContext options I call UseQuerySplittingBehavior(QuerySplittingBehavior.SplitQuery) inside the UseSqlServer configuration. That sets it globally. For any query where I need a single-snapshot JOIN I override it with AsSingleQuery() on that specific query.
If the Shipments list is paginated with Skip and Take, does AsSplitQuery still work correctly?

Yes, but I must have a stable OrderBy before the Skip/Take - without it the separate SELECTs for Items and StatusHistory may not correspond to the same page of Shipments. OrderBy(s => s.Id) before Skip/Take ensures all three queries work on the same ordered set.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅
